flashcards.front: Define Bus Organization and list its three major types in computer systems.
flashcards.back: Bus Organization refers to the structure and arrangement of data pathways connecting CPU, memory, and I/O devices. The three major types are: (1) System Bus (address, data, and control lines), (2) I/O Bus (connects peripherals), and (3) Memory Bus (links CPU to main memory). In DU practicals, the 8085 microprocessor kit demonstrates system bus organization with 8-bit data bus and 16-bit address bus.

flashcards.front: Explain Bus Arbitration and compare Daisy Chain arbitration with Centralized Parallel arbitration.
flashcards.back: Bus Arbitration resolves conflicts when multiple devices request bus access. In Daisy Chain arbitration, devices are connected in series with priority based on physical position (closest to arbiter gets highest priority) - simple but slow. Centralized Parallel arbitration uses separate request/grant lines for each device, allowing simultaneous priority resolution - faster and more scalable but requires more hardware. Delhi University experiments on 8085 use daisy chain for simplicity.

flashcards.front: Differentiate between Synchronous Bus Timing and Asynchronous Bus Timing with examples.
flashcards.back: Synchronous bus uses a central clock to synchronize all operations - simpler design, predictable timing, used in fast transfers (e.g., memory bus, PCI). Asynchronous bus uses handshaking signals (READY, ACK) between devices - more flexible, accommodates devices of varying speeds, used for I/O devices (e.g., ISA bus in older PCs). Example: In 8085 system, synchronous timing controls memory read/write while asynchronous handshaking handles keyboard input.

flashcards.front: What is the Control Unit? Compare Hardwired Control Unit with Microprogrammed Control Unit.
flashcards.back: Control Unit (CU) generates control signals to execute instructions. Hardwired CU uses combinational logic circuits (decoders, state machines) - faster but inflexible, difficult to modify. Microprogrammed CU stores control signals in control memory (ROM/PROM) as microinstructions - flexible, easier to design/modify, but slower due to memory access. For DU syllabus, 8085 uses hardwired design while modern processors use microprogrammed approaches for complex instructions.

flashcards.front: Describe the PCI (Peripheral Component Interconnect) bus features and why it replaced ISA in modern systems.
flashcards.back: PCI is a local bus introduced by Intel (1992) with 32-bit data path, 33/66 MHz clock, supporting plug-and-play, bus mastering, and higher bandwidth (133 MB/s for 32-bit @ 33MHz). It replaced ISA because: (1) ISA limited to 8 MHz with 16-bit width, (2) PCI supports hot-plugging, (3) PCI has built-in configuration space. In DU labs, PCI slots are visible on PC motherboards alongside legacy ISA slots for comparison.

flashcards.front: What is the ISA bus and why is it considered legacy? Compare ISA-8 and ISA-16.
flashcards.back: ISA (Industry Standard Architecture) originated from IBM PC (1981), the original PC bus standard. It's legacy because: limited bandwidth (8 MB/s), fixed I/O address/IRQ assignments, no plug-and-play. ISA-8 is 8-bit bus with 4.77 MHz clock used in XT computers; ISA-16 is 16-bit extended version with 8 MHz clock used in AT computers (1984). Modern DU practical kits still use ISA for parallel port connections to understand legacy interfacing.

flashcards.front: Explain the concepts of Bus Master and Bus Slave with reference to DMA operation.
flashcards.back: Bus Master (or bus arbiter) is a device that initiates bus transactions and controls bus access (e.g., CPU, DMA controller). Bus Slave responds to bus commands from the master (e.g., memory, I/O devices). In DMA (Direct Memory Access), the DMA controller becomes bus master to transfer data directly between I/O and memory without CPU intervention, improving system performance. Example: In DU experiments, memory-to-memory transfer using 8237 DMA controller demonstrates bus mastering.

flashcards.front: What is Microprogramming in Control Unit design? Explain the role of Control Store and Microsequencer.
flashcards.back: Microprogramming is a technique to implement control unit using stored microinstructions. Control Store (ROM/PROM/EPROM) holds microinstruction sequences that generate control signals. Microsequencer determines the sequence of microinstructions to execute, handling branching, looping, and conditional jumps based on status flags. For example, the control unit of 8086 is microprogrammed, where each machine instruction is broken into microoperations stored in control ROM. This allows complex instruction sets and easy modification.
